title: Building the Task Card
intro: You're building the most important UI component in your task manager: the task card. Imagine it like a sticky note on a whiteboard — it needs to show just enough info at a glance without overwhelming. Every design decision you make here affects how users interact with their tasks. Let's see how you approach it.

teach.title: The Task Card Is the Core of Your App
teach.body: The task card is what users see and interact with the most. It needs to display the right information at a glance — title, status, maybe a due date — without being cluttered. Think of it like a physical sticky note: enough info to be useful, but not so much that it's overwhelming.

teach.title: Buttons in an Elevator
teach.body: Imagine pressing an elevator button and nothing happens — no light, no sound, no movement. You'd press it again, harder. Now imagine the button lights up the instant you touch it. You relax because you know the elevator heard you. Interactive UI works the same way: hover effects, active states, and completed styling are the 'lit button' that tells users their action was received.

teach.title: Designing for Interaction
teach.body: A task card isn't just a display — users will click it, complete it, maybe drag it. Build your component with **interaction states** in mind from the start: hover effects, active states, completed styling. A card with no visual feedback when you hover feels broken, even if it technically works.

teach.title: The Task Card Is Like a Business Card
teach.body: A great business card shows your name, title, and one way to reach you — not your entire resume. A task card works the same way: it gives just enough info for someone to decide what to do next. Overload it with details and people's eyes glaze over, just like they would with a business card that has 15 lines of text.

The task card is the heart of your product — it's what users touch dozens of times a day. Nail this component, and you've built something people want to keep coming back to. That's what turns a side project into a money-making app.

Start with the minimum viable card — title and a complete button. Add information (due date, priority, tags) only when your user stories call for it. Every piece of data on the card competes for attention, so be intentional about what you show.

start.narrative: You're creating the TaskCard component. Your designer sent a mockup showing the task title, status, due date, priority, tags, assignee avatar, creation date, description preview, and an action menu. How do you approach building this?

choices.text: Build the full card with all 9 pieces of information from the mockup in one go
Start with just the title and a complete checkbox, then add fields one at a time based on priority
Find a task card component from a UI library and customize it to match the mockup

minimal-start.narrative: Your minimal TaskCard works and looks clean. Now product requirements say you need to show the due date, which can be in the future, today, or overdue. How do you handle the different states?

choices.text: Create a <DueDateBadge> sub-component with conditional Tailwind classes: green for future, yellow for today, red for overdue
Display the date as plain text with no visual distinction between overdue and future dates

overloaded.narrative: The card is packed with information. On mobile, the text is tiny and the layout breaks. Users in testing say they can't quickly scan their task list because every card is a wall of data. What's your fix?

choices.text: Use responsive classes to hide most fields on mobile, only showing title and status
Make the font smaller so everything fits

library-card.narrative: You installed a UI library and customized its Card component. It looks good, but the library's card doesn't support a 'completed' state with strikethrough text, and overriding the library's styles requires fighting its CSS specificity. What do you do?

choices.text: Build your own TaskCard from scratch using the library card as visual reference, but with your own Tailwind styles
Use !important overrides and custom CSS to force the library's card to look right

interaction-states.narrative: Your TaskCard renders, but when users hover or click it, nothing visible happens. The card feels dead — like an elevator button that doesn't light up. How do you add visual feedback?

choices.text: Use Tailwind's hover:, active:, and data-[completed=true]: utilities for state-based styling
Handle onMouseEnter/onMouseLeave with useState to toggle classes manually
Leave it without states — the browser cursor changes and that's enough

accessibility.narrative: A screen reader user reports that your TaskCard announces as 'unnamed button' and they can't tell whether a task is completed. How do you fix accessibility?

choices.text: Use semantic elements (button, h3, time) and add aria-label and aria-pressed where visual context doesn't translate to text
Add a single aria-label='Task' on the root div and call it done
Fix it later — let's finish the MVP features first

testing.narrative: Time to test the component. What's your testing strategy?

choices.text: Behavior tests with React Testing Library: simulate clicks, verify state changes, validate accessibility attributes
Snapshots only — they catch any visual change effortlessly
Skip tests — the component is simple and looks fine when I check manually

excellent-end.narrative: Your TaskCard is a mature component: starts minimal, scales with sub-components, responds to interaction, is screen-reader accessible, and is covered by behavior tests. When another developer modifies it in six months, the tests will tell them immediately if they broke anything.
excellent-end.feedback: This is professional-level component engineering. The decisions you made — start minimal, extract complex logic into sub-components, handle states with Tailwind utilities, semantic accessibility, and behavior tests — are the pillars of components that survive years in production.

mediocre-end.narrative: Your TaskCard works and looks fine. Snapshots catch changes, but when a colleague tweaks padding for a legitimate reason, the snapshot fails and nobody knows whether the change was intentional. The team spends more time updating snapshots than writing new code.
mediocre-end.feedback: You got somewhere functional, but snapshots have a fundamental problem: they validate appearance, not behavior. Good tests verify that 'clicking complete changes the state and announces to the screen reader' — not that the HTML matches pixel for pixel.

no-tests-end.narrative: Your TaskCard looks fine today. In three months, someone refactors state handling and silently breaks accessibility. The bug ships to production until a screen-reader user emails in. You spend a day debugging something a five-line test would have caught instantly.
no-tests-end.feedback: Skipping tests is technical debt that compounds. For a component users touch dozens of times a day, tests aren't a luxury — they're insurance. Start with one test per critical behavior (click complete, show overdue date, accessible announcement) and add more when you fix bugs.
